- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request

### bcg-builtin-redirect-vs-subshell (₢AUAA4) [rough]

## Character

Quick editorial clarification — mechanical, not architectural.

## Docket

BCG Command Substitution Rules currently say: "NO unguarded `$()` — every `$(cmd)` must have `|| buc_die`". This conflates two syntactically similar but semantically distinct forms:

- **`$(command)`** — spawns a subshell, runs a command, captures stdout. Can fail. Guard required.
- **`$(<file)`** — bash builtin file redirect. No subshell, no command. Reads file content directly.

Clarify in BCG that `$(<file)` is a builtin redirect exempt from the `|| buc_die` guard requirement. It is not a command substitution despite sharing the `$()` syntax. Add a note in the Command Substitution Rules section distinguishing the two forms.

### foundry-and-cloud-step-trust-audit (₢AUAA5) [rough]

## Character

Research-heavy with judgment — produces a ranked findings document; no code changes in this pace. Parallels the prior `oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep` review method (₢A_AAN) but widened to the foundry surface, including the step scripts deployed INTO Cloud Build workers (a distinct trust boundary from the on-curia orchestrating bash). Remediation is explicitly deferred: each finding cluster that the operator elects to pursue gets its own follow-up pace, slated by the operator after reviewing the findings.

## Scope

### On-curia orchestrating bash (rbf* family)

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` | Shared foundry primitives (~52 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` | Director build orchestration — conjure/bind/graft/enshrine/inscribe dispatch (~72 KB, largest file in the family) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` | Hallmark ledger — abjure/tally/jettison/rekons/wrest (~36 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh` | Retriever operations — summon, plumb (~12 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` | Verify — vouch emit and consume (~39 KB) |
| `Tools/rbk/rbfc_cli.sh`, `rbfd_cli.sh`, `rbfl_cli.sh`, `rbfr_cli.sh`, `rbfv_cli.sh` | CLI dispatchers |

### Cloud Build step scripts (deployed into Cloud Build workers — distinct trust boundary)

These scripts run INSIDE Cloud Build workers with Cloud Build's identity, not the operator's identity. They receive parameters via `_RBG*` substitutions that the director renders into jq-composed build step bodies. They are a distinct trust surface from the orchestrating bash: quoting hazards land differently (step scripts run in a minimal container); error handling in-builder affects what exit codes the builder surfaces; step scripts see only what the director sends.

| Directory | Count | Role |
|-----------|------:|------|
| `Tools/rbk/rbgja/` | 4 | About assembly — discover-platforms.py, syft-per-platform.sh, build-info-per-platform.py, assemble-push-about.sh |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjb/` | 6 | Buildx — derive-tag-base, qemu-binfmt, buildx-push-image, per-platform-pullback, push-per-platform, push-diags |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgje/` | 1 | Enshrine — copy |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgji/` | 1 | Inscribe — mirror |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjm/` | 1 | Mirror-image |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/` | 3 | Vouch/verify — download-verifier, verify-provenance.py, assemble-push-vouch |

Three of these are Python (`rbgja01`, `rbgja03`, `rbgjv02`) — audited with the same trust-foment lens adapted to Python idioms.

## Method

Apply the same tiered trust-foment framework used in the OAuth review (₢A_AAN), adapted to foundry concerns:

### Tier 1 — visible-in-30-seconds signals

- Shellcheck cleanliness across all foundry bash and step-script bash
- `set -euo pipefail` presence and placement
- Quoting and expansion discipline (especially for parameters that flow into jq, curl, skopeo, docker buildx, oras, etc.)
- Python: lint (ruff/pyflakes if available), `shell=True` subprocess use, unvalidated input
- TODO/FIXME/XXX density, especially undated or in supply-chain-adjacent paths
- Dead code, commented-out blocks, unused constants

### Tier 2 — second-look signals

- **Error handling asymmetry across the rbf* family** — do rbfc, rbfd, rbfl, rbfr, rbfv fail the same way for the same class of problem?
- **Duplication across step scripts** — rbgjb03/rbgjb05 buildx paths, rbgja04/rbgjv03 assemble-push variants
- **Stale references after recent structural work** — with `₢A_AAB` RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX threading and `₢A_AAK` categorical layout changes landing in A_, check for drift, half-migrated paths, or comments that contradict current code
- **TOCTOU / race-adjacent patterns** — temp file creation, permission tightening order
- **Error-swallowing patterns** — `|| true` placements, subshell failure masking, pipefail gaps

### Tier 3 — foundry-specific trust surfaces

1. **Supply-chain chain of custody** — ordain → vouch → summon → plumb. What does each ark promise, and does the consumer actually verify what was promised? Specifically:
   - SLSA provenance: `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` is the verifier. Does the vouch artifact it checks against actually carry what the director claimed to emit?
   - Vouch gate: can a conjure/bind/graft succeed without a matching vouch? Does `rbfr` refuse to summon an un-vouched hallmark?
   - Cross-mode (conjure vs. bind vs. graft): each has different provenance guarantees. Does the mode-aware vouch logic in `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` correctly distinguish, or are there paths where a graft verdict can leak into a conjure-mode verification?

2. **Multi-platform build integrity** — buildx per-platform pullback, digest handling, and platform-specific SBOM/about emission. Does the per-platform pipeline cross-contaminate? Can a push-per-platform step silently push an image built for the wrong platform?

3. **Cloud Build step-script input validation** — step scripts receive `_RBG*` substitutions composed by the director via jq `--arg` and `--argjson`. jq quoting is correct by construction, but:
   - Any place the director uses raw string interpolation into a build YAML/JSON?
   - Any step script that interpolates its own environment into a shell command without quoting?
   - Any step script that accepts an argv parameter without validating its shape?

4. **Cloud Build posture verification** — the step container environment. What's in it? What can it reach? RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc is the stated posture; does step-script behavior match, or does any step script make assumptions incompatible with the stated posture (e.g., expecting network reachability that airgap pool denies)?

5. **Secret handling in step scripts** — step scripts should see only what they need. Check for:
   - Secret Manager refs correctly scoped per step
   - No secrets logged to Cloud Build logs (Google mirrors these; log leaks are persistent)
   - No secrets written to intermediate build artifacts (e.g., baked into image layers)
   - No secrets embedded in step-script source itself

6. **Container-registry write authority** — which step pushes to which path? Can a graft step push into a hallmark path meant for conjure? (The AAK categorical layout tightens this, but worth auditing once it lands.)

7. **Verifier/verified symmetry** — the vouch schema and the provenance-verify schema must stay in lockstep. If `rbgjv03-assemble-push-vouch.sh` emits field X and `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` checks field Y, divergence is a silent trust gap.

8. **Dependency pins inside step scripts** — step scripts pull tools (syft, skopeo, oras, cosign if present, docker buildx, qemu-binfmt, gcloud-within-builder). Are these pinned by digest? By tag? Not at all? The RBSRG (RegimeGcbPins) spec is the reference contract; does reality match?

9. **Failure modes visible to operator** — when a cloud build step fails, what does the operator see? Cryptic exit codes, clean structured errors, or a wall of unattributed output? This is a trust signal for the operator experience more than for an external auditor, but both matter.

10. **Python ↔ bash boundary** — `rbgja01-discover-platforms.py`, `rbgja03-build-info-per-platform.py`, `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py`. These read bash-set environment, emit JSON to stdout or files, and signal failure via exit code. The boundary is where most cross-language bugs live. Check: JSON emission discipline (one doc per invocation, no stray stdout), exit-code fidelity, schema stability.

## Deliverable

A findings report written to the chat (not committed to disk unless operator requests) matching the format produced for ₢A_AAN:

- **"What builds trust here"** section — specific patterns worth preserving (setting the floor)
- **Tier 1 / 2 / 3 findings** — each numbered (F1, F2, …), each naming file:line, describing the trust-foment signal, and proposing a specific fix or clarification
- **Priority list** — findings ranked by trust-yield-per-line-of-diff so the operator can pick which to pursue first

The operator then reviews and elects which clusters to remediate. Each elected cluster becomes its own follow-up pace, either in AU (if pre-release scope) or in A_ (if structural enough to block depot regen). F-numbering is internal to this audit and does not carry across pace boundaries.

## Relation to other work

- **₢A_AAN** (oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep) already covers the OAuth surface (`rbgo_OAuth.sh`, `rbgp_Payor.sh` OAuth portion, `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`, `rbro_regime.sh`, `rbro_cli.sh`). This foundry audit does NOT revisit those files.
- **F7 follow-up** (factor three token-exchange code paths, still to be slated in A_) is also OAuth-scoped and not revisited here.
- **AAK / AAL / AAB** (GAR categorical layout and prefix threading in A_) are actively reshaping paths the foundry constructs. The audit acknowledges this: findings about path construction should be cross-referenced against AAK's helper-module intent (`rbgl_GarLayout.sh`) to avoid flagging things AAK already plans to fix. Ideally this audit runs AFTER AAK lands, so the audit reviews the post-migration state rather than transitional code.
- **Recent AU paces** touched `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (lanes S/A/J/K/F — build provenance, multiplatform, vouch preflight, digest extraction) and `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` (lane F). The audit reads the current state; it does not revisit decisions those paces made, only flags if the implementations embody trust-foment signals visible at the code surface.

## Out of scope

- Implementing any fixes (separate per-cluster paces — operator's decision)
- Auditing OAuth code (covered by ₢A_AAN and its forthcoming F7 sibling)
- Auditing non-foundry / non-step-script RBK code (governor, IAM, access-probe — separate audits if wanted)
- Auditing JJK, BUK, CMK, VVK — all out-of-kit scope
- Pen-testing the Cloud Build environment itself (that's an external-service posture question — see RBSCB; audit only compares script behavior to stated posture)
- Changing the stated Cloud Build posture (RBSCB is a design decision; audit observes, doesn't propose)
- Regenerating pinned digest references (RBSRG is a contract; audit checks adherence, doesn't rotate)

## Verification (for the audit pace itself)

This is a research pace; the "verification" is that the deliverable exists, names real file:line references, is internally consistent, and the operator reads it and either accepts it as-is or asks for clarifications before deciding on follow-ups.

- Every finding names `file:line` (verifiable by inspection)
- Every finding proposes a specific remediation OR explicitly marks "observation-only"
- Findings are ranked (not a flat list)
- Priority table clusters related findings so follow-up paces can be scoped cleanly
- "What builds trust here" section present — audits that only find faults foment their own kind of distrust

### factor-openssl-base64-helpers (₢AUAA6) [rough]

## Character
Deferred decision — slate now, judge later.

## Problem
`openssl enc -base64 ... -A` duplicated across 6 sites with no load-bearing variation. AUAAr cycle 4 showed the cost: missed `-A` on one site during standardization, dormant ~26 days, surfaces only on live API path.

## Sites
- Decode: `rbgg_Governor.sh:242`, `rbgp_Payor.sh:1355`, `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:959`, `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1187`
- Encode: `rbgo_OAuth.sh:69`, `rbgo_OAuth.sh:131`

## Proposal
Thin helpers in `rbgu_Utility.sh`:
- `zrbgu_base64_decode_file_to_file(input, output)`
- `zrbgu_base64_decode_string_to_file(b64, output)`
- Matching encode pair; `rbgo_OAuth.sh:69` is legitimately a `_capture` (returns string).

Not `_capture` for the decode pair — output is a file, not a stdout value.

## Done

## Character

## Problem
Calling every regime render/info function as a prerelease check currently fails. Many functions die with unhelpful errors ("Module X not kindled", unbound variable crashes) instead of actionable messages. Some renders produce verbose logging inappropriate for a sweep context.

## Deliverable A: Repair error messages across all regime renders

Fix every render/validate/info function so that "failure to have parameter" messages are helpful and correct.

### Specific failures to repair

**Folio-gated renders** (`rbrn_render`, `rbra_render`, `rbrv_render`):
- Die with terse messages when `BUZ_FOLIO` is empty

**`rbro_render`** (Tools/rbk/rbro_cli.sh):

**`rbob_info`** (Tools/rbk/rbob_cli.sh):
- Crashes on unbound `ZRBOB_*` variables if module not kindled

**Sentinel messages generally:**
- "Module X not kindled - call zX_kindle first" assumes internal knowledge

### bcg-builtin-redirect-vs-subshell (₢AUAA4) [rough]

## Character

Quick editorial clarification — mechanical, not architectural.

## Docket

BCG Command Substitution Rules currently say: "NO unguarded `$()` — every `$(cmd)` must have `|| buc_die`". This conflates two syntactically similar but semantically distinct forms:

- **`$(command)`** — spawns a subshell, runs a command, captures stdout. Can fail. Guard required.
- **`$(<file)`** — bash builtin file redirect. No subshell, no command. Reads file content directly.

Clarify in BCG that `$(<file)` is a builtin redirect exempt from the `|| buc_die` guard requirement. It is not a command substitution despite sharing the `$()` syntax. Add a note in the Command Substitution Rules section distinguishing the two forms.

These scripts run INSIDE Cloud Build workers with Cloud Build's identity, not the operator's identity. They receive parameters via `_RBG*` substitutions that the director renders into jq-composed build step bodies. They are a distinct trust surface from the orchestrating bash: quoting hazards land differently (step scripts run in a minimal container); error handling in-builder affects what exit codes the builder surfaces; step scripts see only what the director sends.

| Directory | Count | Role |
|-----------|------:|------|
| `Tools/rbk/rbgja/` | 4 | About assembly — discover-platforms.py, syft-per-platform.sh, build-info-per-platform.py, assemble-push-about.sh |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjb/` | 6 | Buildx — derive-tag-base, qemu-binfmt, buildx-push-image, per-platform-pullback, push-per-platform, push-diags |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgje/` | 1 | Enshrine — copy |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgji/` | 1 | Inscribe — mirror |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjm/` | 1 | Mirror-image |
| `Tools/rbk/rbgjv/` | 3 | Vouch/verify — download-verifier, verify-provenance.py, assemble-push-vouch |

Three of these are Python (`rbgja01`, `rbgja03`, `rbgjv02`) — audited with the same trust-foment lens adapted to Python idioms.

## Method

Apply the same tiered trust-foment framework used in the OAuth review (₢A_AAN), adapted to foundry concerns:

### Tier 1 — visible-in-30-seconds signals

- Shellcheck cleanliness across all foundry bash and step-script bash
- `set -euo pipefail` presence and placement
- Quoting and expansion discipline (especially for parameters that flow into jq, curl, skopeo, docker buildx, oras, etc.)
- Python: lint (ruff/pyflakes if available), `shell=True` subprocess use, unvalidated input
- TODO/FIXME/XXX density, especially undated or in supply-chain-adjacent paths
- Dead code, commented-out blocks, unused constants

### Tier 2 — second-look signals

- **Error handling asymmetry across the rbf* family** — do rbfc, rbfd, rbfl, rbfr, rbfv fail the same way for the same class of problem?
- **Duplication across step scripts** — rbgjb03/rbgjb05 buildx paths, rbgja04/rbgjv03 assemble-push variants
- **Stale references after recent structural work** — with `₢A_AAB` RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX threading and `₢A_AAK` categorical layout changes landing in A_, check for drift, half-migrated paths, or comments that contradict current code
- **TOCTOU / race-adjacent patterns** — temp file creation, permission tightening order
- **Error-swallowing patterns** — `|| true` placements, subshell failure masking, pipefail gaps

### Tier 3 — foundry-specific trust surfaces

1. **Supply-chain chain of custody** — ordain → vouch → summon → plumb. What does each ark promise, and does the consumer actually verify what was promised? Specifically:
   - SLSA provenance: `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` is the verifier. Does the vouch artifact it checks against actually carry what the director claimed to emit?
   - Vouch gate: can a conjure/bind/graft succeed without a matching vouch? Does `rbfr` refuse to summon an un-vouched hallmark?
   - Cross-mode (conjure vs. bind vs. graft): each has different provenance guarantees. Does the mode-aware vouch logic in `rbfv_FoundryVerify.sh` correctly distinguish, or are there paths where a graft verdict can leak into a conjure-mode verification?

2. **Multi-platform build integrity** — buildx per-platform pullback, digest handling, and platform-specific SBOM/about emission. Does the per-platform pipeline cross-contaminate? Can a push-per-platform step silently push an image built for the wrong platform?

3. **Cloud Build step-script input validation** — step scripts receive `_RBG*` substitutions composed by the director via jq `--arg` and `--argjson`. jq quoting is correct by construction, but:
   - Any place the director uses raw string interpolation into a build YAML/JSON?
   - Any step script that interpolates its own environment into a shell command without quoting?
   - Any step script that accepts an argv parameter without validating its shape?

4. **Cloud Build posture verification** — the step container environment. What's in it? What can it reach? RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc is the stated posture; does step-script behavior match, or does any step script make assumptions incompatible with the stated posture (e.g., expecting network reachability that airgap pool denies)?

5. **Secret handling in step scripts** — step scripts should see only what they need. Check for:
   - Secret Manager refs correctly scoped per step
   - No secrets logged to Cloud Build logs (Google mirrors these; log leaks are persistent)
   - No secrets written to intermediate build artifacts (e.g., baked into image layers)
   - No secrets embedded in step-script source itself

6. **Container-registry write authority** — which step pushes to which path? Can a graft step push into a hallmark path meant for conjure? (The AAK categorical layout tightens this, but worth auditing once it lands.)

7. **Verifier/verified symmetry** — the vouch schema and the provenance-verify schema must stay in lockstep. If `rbgjv03-assemble-push-vouch.sh` emits field X and `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` checks field Y, divergence is a silent trust gap.

8. **Dependency pins inside step scripts** — step scripts pull tools (syft, skopeo, oras, cosign if present, docker buildx, qemu-binfmt, gcloud-within-builder). Are these pinned by digest? By tag? Not at all? The RBSRG (RegimeGcbPins) spec is the reference contract; does reality match?

9. **Failure modes visible to operator** — when a cloud build step fails, what does the operator see? Cryptic exit codes, clean structured errors, or a wall of unattributed output? This is a trust signal for the operator experience more than for an external auditor, but both matter.

10. **Python ↔ bash boundary** — `rbgja01-discover-platforms.py`, `rbgja03-build-info-per-platform.py`, `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py`. These read bash-set environment, emit JSON to stdout or files, and signal failure via exit code. The boundary is where most cross-language bugs live. Check: JSON emission discipline (one doc per invocation, no stray stdout), exit-code fidelity, schema stability.

## Deliverable

A findings report written to the chat (not committed to disk unless operator requests) matching the format produced for ₢A_AAN:

- **"What builds trust here"** section — specific patterns worth preserving (setting the floor)
- **Tier 1 / 2 / 3 findings** — each numbered (F1, F2, …), each naming file:line, describing the trust-foment signal, and proposing a specific fix or clarification
- **Priority list** — findings ranked by trust-yield-per-line-of-diff so the operator can pick which to pursue first

The operator then reviews and elects which clusters to remediate. Each elected cluster becomes its own follow-up pace, either in AU (if pre-release scope) or in A_ (if structural enough to block depot regen). F-numbering is internal to this audit and does not carry across pace boundaries.

## Relation to other work

- **₢A_AAN** (oauth-trust-hygiene-sweep) already covers the OAuth surface (`rbgo_OAuth.sh`, `rbgp_Payor.sh` OAuth portion, `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`, `rbro_regime.sh`, `rbro_cli.sh`). This foundry audit does NOT revisit those files.
- **F7 follow-up** (factor three token-exchange code paths, still to be slated in A_) is also OAuth-scoped and not revisited here.
- **AAK / AAL / AAB** (GAR categorical layout and prefix threading in A_) are actively reshaping paths the foundry constructs. The audit acknowledges this: findings about path construction should be cross-referenced against AAK's helper-module intent (`rbgl_GarLayout.sh`) to avoid flagging things AAK already plans to fix. Ideally this audit runs AFTER AAK lands, so the audit reviews the post-migration state rather than transitional code.
- **Recent AU paces** touched `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` (lanes S/A/J/K/F — build provenance, multiplatform, vouch preflight, digest extraction) and `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` (lane F). The audit reads the current state; it does not revisit decisions those paces made, only flags if the implementations embody trust-foment signals visible at the code surface.

## Out of scope

- Implementing any fixes (separate per-cluster paces — operator's decision)
- Auditing OAuth code (covered by ₢A_AAN and its forthcoming F7 sibling)
- Auditing non-foundry / non-step-script RBK code (governor, IAM, access-probe — separate audits if wanted)
- Auditing JJK, BUK, CMK, VVK — all out-of-kit scope
- Pen-testing the Cloud Build environment itself (that's an external-service posture question — see RBSCB; audit only compares script behavior to stated posture)
- Changing the stated Cloud Build posture (RBSCB is a design decision; audit observes, doesn't propose)
- Regenerating pinned digest references (RBSRG is a contract; audit checks adherence, doesn't rotate)

## Verification (for the audit pace itself)

This is a research pace; the "verification" is that the deliverable exists, names real file:line references, is internally consistent, and the operator reads it and either accepts it as-is or asks for clarifications before deciding on follow-ups.

- Every finding names `file:line` (verifiable by inspection)
- Every finding proposes a specific remediation OR explicitly marks "observation-only"
- Findings are ranked (not a flat list)
- Priority table clusters related findings so follow-up paces can be scoped cleanly
- "What builds trust here" section present — audits that only find faults foment their own kind of distrust

### factor-openssl-base64-helpers (₢AUAA6) [rough]

## Character
Deferred decision — slate now, judge later.

## Problem
`openssl enc -base64 ... -A` duplicated across 6 sites with no load-bearing variation. AUAAr cycle 4 showed the cost: missed `-A` on one site during standardization, dormant ~26 days, surfaces only on live API path.

## Sites
- Decode: `rbgg_Governor.sh:242`, `rbgp_Payor.sh:1355`, `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:959`, `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1187`
- Encode: `rbgo_OAuth.sh:69`, `rbgo_OAuth.sh:131`

## Proposal
Thin helpers in `rbgu_Utility.sh`:
- `zrbgu_base64_decode_file_to_file(input, output)`
- `zrbgu_base64_decode_string_to_file(b64, output)`
- Matching encode pair; `rbgo_OAuth.sh:69` is legitimately a `_capture` (returns string).

Not `_capture` for the decode pair — output is a file, not a stdout value.

## Done
All 6 sites call helpers; `openssl enc -base64` lives only in `rbgu_Utility.sh`. OR: closed won't-fix with rationale recorded.

## Reference
Commit f3cd71c4 (AUAAr cycle 4 fault analysis).

MVP-2 (₣As, racing) is unifying IAM grant implementation. MVP-3 picks up the remaining release concerns: supply chain trust gaps, developer qualification tooling, and consumer-facing documentation.

# jjezs_paddock ₣AU

# Paddock: rbk-mvp-3-release-finalize

## Context

Harden the Recipe Bottle supply chain trust model and complete release-readiness tooling. MVP-1 (₣Ak, retired) established the core build/test/deploy pipeline, directory restructure (Tools/rbk/, vov_veiled), and specification foundations. MVP-2 (₣As, racing) is unifying IAM grant implementation. MVP-3 picks up the remaining release concerns: supply chain trust gaps, developer qualification tooling, and consumer-facing documentation.

## What MVP-1 Already Accomplished

- Renamed Tools/rbw/ to Tools/rbk/
- Moved 57 RBS* specs from lenses/ to Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/
- Deleted rbk_Coordinator.sh, cleaned rbw.workbench.mk references
- Established test sweep infrastructure and full qualification gate
- SLSA provenance verification via vouch pipeline

## MVP-3 Themes

**Supply chain trust hardening**: Summon now pulls -vouch artifact (landed). Remaining: make -vouch multi-platform (AUAAJ), then require local -vouch presence on every bottle start as unconditional preflight gate (AUAAK).

**Developer tooling**: Qualification split into fast and release tiers (AUAAI, done). Fast qualify (rbw-Qf) runs on bottle-start: tabtargets, colophons, nameplate preflight — no shellcheck. Release qualify (rbw-QR) adds shellcheck + complete test suite. Regime validation testbench added (AUAAE, done). Remaining: manifest digest simplification (AUAAF), consecration inspect command (AUAAL), regime variable completeness check (AUAAH).

**Release prep**: Consumer-facing documentation pipeline: consolidate lenses into veiled (AUAAO), consumer CLAUDE.md template (AUAAN), freshen cosmology and render index.html (AUAAP), getting-started README (AUAAQ). Final: prep-release slash command ceremony (AUAAR) that orchestrates qualification, branch mechanics, content stripping, CLAUDE.md swap, and marshal reset.

## BCG Compliance Audit Results (₢AUAAZ)

### Wave 1 (bd6eba19) — correctness/portability tier
- 5 pseudo-ternary `A && B || C` patterns → if/then/else or inverted tests
- 11 non-POSIX `[[ == ]]` → `test =`
- ~40 `[ ]` bracket tests → `test` command
- 2 `eval` → `${!name}` indirect expansion
- 2 `eval` hardened with variable name validation
- 6 `echo -e` → `printf '%b\n'`

### Wave 2 (627e1c0f) — structural/error-handling tier
- Renamed `zbuc_do_execute` → `zbuc_doc_mode_predicate` (non-predicate in if, 8 sites)
- `[[ ]]` → `test` in bash version checks (2 sites)
- Split combined `local z_var=$(cmd)` → two-line capture pattern
- Added `|| buc_die` to 10 unguarded mkdir/cp/rm/date commands
- `head -1` → `read -r` builtin (2 sites)
- Heredoc → echo sequence (1 site)
- `dirname` → `${var%/*}` parameter expansion (1 site)
- Added `readonly` to kindle array ZRBRR_DOCKER_ENV

### Remaining: command substitution elimination (~20 violations, 8 files)
BCG rule: NO `$(command)` except `$(<file)` and `_capture` functions.
Each needs temp file path (kindle constant), _capture wrapper, or builtin replacement.
Docket reslated with per-file inventory.

### Deferred Technical Debt (itch for post-release)
- **z_ prefix on locals**: 1,519 occurrences across 63 files — naming convention, no runtime impact
- **Unbraced expansions `"$var"`**: 198 occurrences across 49 files — mechanical
- **`2>/dev/null` stderr suppression**: 73 occurrences across 20 files — case-by-case judgment
- **`local -r` missing**: Pervasive — single-assignment locals without readonly
- **Raw echo for user messages**: 200+ in display-heavy modules (rbf inspect, rbj sentry, rbo observe)
- **Bare file truncation** (`> file` without `:`): 38 occurrences, 7 files

content.text:
# Groom: ₣AU — rbk-14-mvp-release-finalize (racing)

**Heat shape:** This is MVP-3, the release-readiness phase. MVP-1 (₣Ak, retired) built the core pipeline and directory restructure; MVP-2 (₣As) unified IAM grants. ₣AU picks up the *remaining* release concerns — supply-chain trust hardening, developer qualification tooling, and consumer-facing docs. A lot of the original MVP-3 work (vouch multi-platform, qualify tiers, regime testbench, cosmology refresh) has already landed. What's left is **7 open paces**, all still `[rough]` (no firm execution commitment yet).

Here's what each open pace is actually about, in plain terms:

---

**1. ₢AUAAh — prerelease-regime-render-sweep**
Right now, if you call every regime render/info function as a pre-release sanity check, many of them die ugly — "Module X not kindled", `set -u` unbound-variable crashes (notably `rbro_render` on `RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET`, and `rbob_info` on `ZRBOB_*`) — instead of giving actionable "you forgot parameter Y" messages. Three deliverables: (A) repair those error messages across all 11 regime renders, (B) add logging-quiet discipline so a batch sweep isn't noisy, (C) build a test fixture that sources every regime file and calls every render/info, asserting exit 0 on each. Mechanical with judgment on the logging part.

**2. ₢AUAAR — prep-release-slash-command**
The `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` ceremony is currently a foot-gun — it would fail at Step 8 because it copies a file that's since been retired. This pace brings the whole ceremony current against three structural changes that landed after it was written (retired `index.html`/`README.consumer.md`/`.nojekyll`; the CLAUDE.consumer.md glossary swap; the `rbw-h*`→`rbw-f*` foundry colophon restructure). Five tracks: retirement-artifact cleanup, command-audit automation in `rbq_qualify_release`, verifying three "claimed-done" items, fixing the `OPEN_SOURCE_UPSTREAM` git remote (needs your confirmation — shared state), and a final verification sweep. This is the pace that gates the *first actual release attempt*.

**3. ₢AUAA1 — relocate-compose-probe-env**
Small cleanup. `.rbk/rbje_compose_probe.env` is a test fixture / sentinel (a compose env-file quoting probe), but it's sitting in the `.rbk/` *runtime config* directory next to real regime files like `rbrp.env`. Move it into `Tools/rbk/` somewhere appropriate and update all references (compose files, sentry startup, anything sourcing it). Exact destination decided during execution.

**4. ₢AUAA4 — bcg-builtin-redirect-vs-subshell**
Quick doc edit. BCG's command-substitution rule says every `$(cmd)` must have `|| buc_die`. But `$(<file)` is a bash *builtin redirect* — no subshell, no command, can't fail that way — so the guard requirement shouldn't apply to it. Add a note in the BCG Command Substitution Rules section distinguishing the two forms. Editorial, not architectural.

**5. ₢AUAA5 — foundry-and-cloud-step-trust-audit**
The big one. A research-only audit (no code changes) of the foundry surface, mirroring the earlier OAuth trust-hygiene sweep method but widened to cover both the on-curia orchestrating bash (the `rbf*` family) *and* the Cloud Build step scripts that run *inside* Cloud Build workers (a distinct trust boundary — `rbgj*` dirs, including 3 Python scripts). Three tiers of analysis culminating in a ranked findings report (F1, F2…) with file:line refs and proposed fixes. Remediation is explicitly deferred — each cluster you elect to pursue becomes its own follow-up pace. Note: the docket says this ideally runs *after* the A_ GAR-layout work (AAK) lands, so it audits post-migration state.

**6. ₢AUAA6 — factor-openssl-base64-helpers**
`openssl enc -base64 ... -A` is duplicated across 6 sites with no real variation. A prior cycle proved the cost: a missed `-A` on one site sat dormant ~26 days and only surfaced on the live API path. Proposal: thin encode/decode helpers in `rbgu_Utility.sh` so `openssl enc -base64` lives in exactly one place. Marked "deferred decision — slate now, judge later"; the "done" state explicitly allows a won't-fix-with-rationale outcome.

**7. ₢AUAA7 — run-all-onboarding-tracks**
End-to-end validation from the user's perspective. Walk every handbook onboarding track in sequence — Crash Course, Credential Retriever, Credential Director, First Crucible, Director First Cloud Build, Payor, Governor — confirm every window renders correctly with prefixed resource names, probe outputs show the right prefixed container/image identifiers, and fix any display or functional breakage you hit.

---

## How they relate

There's a natural ordering hiding in here:

- **₢AUAA4** (BCG doc) and **₢AUAA1** (move probe env) are quick, independent, low-risk — easy wins anytime.
- **₢AUAAh** (regime-render repair) is a prerequisite-feeling pace: clean render errors make the pre-release sweep trustworthy.
- **₢AUAA5** (foundry audit) wants to run *after* the A_ AAK layout work, and may *spawn* new remediation paces — so it's more of a fork in the road than a checkbox.
- **₢AUAA6** (base64 helpers) is a deferred maybe.
- **₢AUAAR** (prep-release ceremony) and **₢AUAA7** (onboarding walk) are the genuine release gates — they should land near the *end*, after the surface stops moving.

Want me to do anything structural here — rail them into that execution order, reslate any docket that's drifted, or mount one to start? Or was this just the memory-refresh you were after?

## Staleness assessment

I checked the drift-prone claims directly rather than trusting age. Results are uneven — and two paces appear to have quietly become moot.

- **It predates a heat renumbering.** Its title is `rbk-mvp-3-release-finalize`; the heat's actual silks is `rbk-14-mvp-release-finalize`. The whole "MVP-1/MVP-2/MVP-3" framing is from before heats got numbered into the 09–30 range.
- **`₣As` is gone.** The paddock says "MVP-2 (₣As, racing)" — but `₣As` isn't in the muster at all anymore (retired/archived). That reference is dead.
- **Scope may have been absorbed.** There's now a separate racing heat `₣BB rbk-16-mvp-release-qualification` (55/64). Its name overlaps heavily with AU's stated "developer qualification tooling / release qualification" theme. Worth deciding whether AU's charter still holds or has partly migrated to BB.
- **Coronet refs litter the prose** — `AUAAJ, AUAAK, AUAAI, AUAAE, AUAAF, AUAAL, AUAAH, AUAAO, AUAAN, AUAAP, AUAAQ, AUAAR`. JJK guidance is explicit that coronets don't belong in paddock prose (pace-state ops silently invalidate them), and several of these are closed.
- **Dead theme**: it lists "render index.html (AUAAP)" as a live concern, but `index.html` was retired (commit b7c1dbdd) — as AUAAR's own docket documents.

- **₢AUAA1 (relocate-compose-probe-env)** — **likely complete.** The file is no longer in `.rbk/`; it's already sitting at `Tools/rbk/rbje_compose_probe.env`, which is exactly the move the docket asks for. ## Paces

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift

### docker-login-retry-shape (₢BBAA8) [rough]

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.

## Issue evidence

Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:

    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)

Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.

## Asymmetry to close

`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.

## Scope

Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).

## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing

Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.

- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class

- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### fast-qualify-shellcheck-fold-cover-zero (₢BBABG) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical. Fold the two fast-codebase-check entry points into one tier, parallelize the slow one, expand its coverage, bring its baseline to zero, and wire it into marshal-zero as an early structural gate.

## Locked decisions

- **One fast-tier shellcheck entry.** `rbq_qualify_fast` absorbs `buq_shellcheck`; standalone `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` retires. The primitive function stays; only the standalone tabtarget retires. `rbw-tr` drops its now-redundant shellcheck phase.
- **Parallelize via xargs -P.** Replace the single shellcheck invocation with xargs-driven parallel invocation; aggregate exit code into one verdict. Target ~1–2s wall-clock for the file set (down from ~9s).
- **Coverage expansion.** `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` joins the shellcheck file set. Mount-time call on scope-generalization shape (multi-root parameter vs explicit outside-Tools list).
- **Baseline zero.** Every current shellcheck finding either fixed in-place or moved to `busc_shellcheckrc` with BCG-structural rationale. Fast-tier exits zero on clean tree after wrap.
- **MZ early gate.** `rblm_zero` runs the parallelized shellcheck primitive in its pre-check block (alongside tree-clean / upstream / pushed). Non-zero shellcheck fails marshal-zero before it touches anything. Gauntlet entry-state cannot accept a shellcheck-dirty codebase by construction.

## Wrap criterion

`tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` exits zero, runs in ~2s wall-clock, covers `Tools/` plus `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh`. `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` no longer exists. `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` no longer makes a separate shellcheck call. `tt/rbw-MZ.MarshalZeroes.sh` fails fast on any shellcheck finding before mutating anything.

## Out of scope

- `tt/*.sh` shellcheck coverage (`buq_tabtargets` shape-check is the appropriate gate for generated shims)
- `.buk/launcher.*.sh` (3–4 line trivial shims; capture as itch if ever produces a finding)
- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/` subtree — operator call at mount (exempt vs fix)

## Character

## Source
`Memos/memo-20260527-account-state-invalid-reinvest-flap.md` — full 401/`ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` signature, two-host evidence, the prior-art gap, and the time-bound tolerance model to reuse.

## Shape (locked)
- `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` build-status poll loop: absorb a transient `401 UNAUTHENTICATED` + `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` with bounded backoff instead of counting it toward the consecutive-error fatal — a still-`WORKING` build must survive a director-SA auth flap.
- `rbgo_OAuth.sh` consumer-retry token mint: extend the existing retry error-class to `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`, covering the `rbfv` vouch path (Host B in the memo).
- The retry budget is the discriminator (settling completes within budget; real denial waits it out and fails clean) — reuse the established propagation / access-probe time-bound model rather than minting new literals.

## Done
A director re-invest immediately followed by `ordain` survives the flap: the transient is logged and retried within budget, never fatal on a healthy build. Confirm the `rbfv` vouch failure body is the same class (memo caveat).

## Paces

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios

Branch isolation lets parallel attempts on different machines avoid the commit-trail race on main; with BBABB landed, parallel mac+linux execution against a shared payor manor is now first-class — distinct BURS_TINCTURE values give every RBDC-derived name (depot project_id, GAR repo, GCS bucket, SA emails) per-station disjointness by construction.

### docker-login-retry-shape (₢BBAA8) [rough]

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.

## Issue evidence

Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:

    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)

Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.

## Asymmetry to close

`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.

## Scope

Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).

## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing

Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.

## Out of scope

- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
- Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires

## References

- moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
- BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

## Character

Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.

Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.

Update README "Release Procedure" section to reflect that the prep-release step now enforces rbw-tP had to pass against the very HEAD that prep-release is preparing. README references the post-success cleanup ceremony documented in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI).

## Known sync points (running list — append as paces land)

- **`RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`** (from BBAAc inline fix on the JWT-SA probe — IAM-policy-read propagation race absorption). The procedural spec describes the OLD semantic: "all iterations must pass; HTTP 403 → fatal access denied; HTTP 401 → fatal token rejected." New code semantic in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`: bounded propagation-absorbing retry — first 2xx wins, HTTP 403 retries within `max_attempts × delay_ms` budget, all other codes (including 401) fall to a single fail-fast catch-all with body-summary diagnostic via `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture`. Sections needing rewrite: §"Execute Probe Iterations" (rename concept to *attempts*; rewrite "Evaluate Response" sub-section to reflect 2xx-wins / 403-retry / else-fatal); §"Display Summary" ("succeeded after «I» attempt(s)" instead of "«N»/«N» iterations passed"); the top NOTE block (intent shift from sustained-access verification to propagation-window absorption + verification); §"Validate Input Parameters" (`ITERATION_COUNT` description shifts to "max attempts — first 2xx wins"; `DELAY_MS` to "between retries"). Companion call-site change: `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` defaults shifted to 60 × 3000ms (≈180s budget) for SA roles only; Payor probe defaults and semantic unchanged.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### fast-qualify-shellcheck-fold-cover-zero (₢BBABG) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical. Fold the two fast-codebase-check entry points into one tier, parallelize the slow one, expand its coverage, bring its baseline to zero, and wire it into marshal-zero as an early structural gate.

## Locked decisions

- **One fast-tier shellcheck entry.** `rbq_qualify_fast` absorbs `buq_shellcheck`; standalone `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` retires. The primitive function stays; only the standalone tabtarget retires. `rbw-tr` drops its now-redundant shellcheck phase.
- **Parallelize via xargs -P.** Replace the single shellcheck invocation with xargs-driven parallel invocation; aggregate exit code into one verdict. Target ~1–2s wall-clock for the file set (down from ~9s).
- **Coverage expansion.** `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh` joins the shellcheck file set. Mount-time call on scope-generalization shape (multi-root parameter vs explicit outside-Tools list).
- **Baseline zero.** Every current shellcheck finding either fixed in-place or moved to `busc_shellcheckrc` with BCG-structural rationale. Fast-tier exits zero on clean tree after wrap.
- **MZ early gate.** `rblm_zero` runs the parallelized shellcheck primitive in its pre-check block (alongside tree-clean / upstream / pushed). Non-zero shellcheck fails marshal-zero before it touches anything. Gauntlet entry-state cannot accept a shellcheck-dirty codebase by construction.

## Wrap criterion

`tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh` exits zero, runs in ~2s wall-clock, covers `Tools/` plus `.buk/rbbc_constants.sh`. `tt/buw-qsc.QualifyShellCheck.sh` no longer exists. `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` no longer makes a separate shellcheck call. `tt/rbw-MZ.MarshalZeroes.sh` fails fast on any shellcheck finding before mutating anything.

## Out of scope

- `tt/*.sh` shellcheck coverage (`buq_tabtargets` shape-check is the appropriate gate for generated shims)
- `.buk/launcher.*.sh` (3–4 line trivial shims; capture as itch if ever produces a finding)
- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/` subtree — operator call at mount (exempt vs fix)

### foundry-account-state-invalid-tolerance (₢BBABL) [rough]

## Character
Mechanical retry-tolerance extension; shape locked by the memo. Two touchpoints, one error class. Surfaced by dogfight verification (2 failures / 3 runs — transient).

## Source
`Memos/memo-20260527-account-state-invalid-reinvest-flap.md` — full 401/`ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` signature, two-host evidence, the prior-art gap, and the time-bound tolerance model to reuse.

## Shape (locked)
- `rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` build-status poll loop: absorb a transient `401 UNAUTHENTICATED` + `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` with bounded backoff instead of counting it toward the consecutive-error fatal — a still-`WORKING` build must survive a director-SA auth flap.
- `rbgo_OAuth.sh` consumer-retry token mint: extend the existing retry error-class to `ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID`, covering the `rbfv` vouch path (Host B in the memo).
- The retry budget is the discriminator (settling completes within budget; real denial waits it out and fails clean) — reuse the established propagation / access-probe time-bound model rather than minting new literals.

## Done
A director re-invest immediately followed by `ordain` survives the flap: the transient is logged and retried within budget, never fatal on a healthy build. Confirm the `rbfv` vouch failure body is the same class (memo caveat).

Checked it — and yes, ₣BB materially changes the picture for ₣AU. Here's what I found.

## ₣BB — rbk-16-mvp-release-qualification (racing, 55/64, created 260426)

**₣BB is the live successor to ₣AU's release charter.** ₣AU was created 260131; ₣BB came three months later (260426) and is far more developed — 64 paces, a full marshal-zero → `rbw-tP` gauntlet/pristine model, a `RELEASE.md` runbook, and 339 commits of foundry/qualification reshaping. ₣AU's "MVP-1/2/3" paddock framing predates ₣BB's existence entirely.

The overlaps are concrete, not vibes:

### 1. Direct file collision: the prep-release ceremony (sharpest)

- **₢AUAAR** (AU) rewrites `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` to fix stale references (the retired `index.html`, `README.consumer.md`, `.nojekyll`).
- **₢BBAAH** (BB, prep-release-integration) *also* edits `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — to wire a pristine-pass precondition in.

Neither has landed (I confirmed the file is still the old 257-line Steps 0–13 version with the foot-gun intact). And critically, **₢BBAAH's docket says it preserves "the rest of /rbk-prep-release as-is"** — i.e. it *assumes* the stale parts are already cleaned up. That's exactly ₢AUAAR's job. So they're complementary in substance but split across two heats with no coordination. This is the clearest case for consolidation: ₢AUAAR's cleanup is really a prerequisite for ₢BBAAH, and both belong under one owner — naturally ₣BB, which owns the release ceremony now.

### 2. ₢AUAA6 (base64 helpers) — premise already shifted

₣BB landed a **`base64-to-openssl-sweep`** pace. That's why AUAA6's line numbers drifted — BB unified all six sites on `openssl`. AUAA6's remaining work (factor the unified invocation into `rbgu_Utility.sh` helpers) is still undone and still valid, but its founding rationale (commit f3cd71c4, "AUAAr cycle 4") is now pre-BB history. Worth re-reading against the post-sweep state.

### 3. ₢AUAA5 (foundry trust audit) — target heavily reshaped

₣BB did massive foundry work — `rbfd`, `rbfc`, `rbfl`, `rbfv`, `rbfr` all reshaped, plus dockerfile-hygiene, account-state-invalid-tolerance, cult-verb refactors. AUAA5's docket already says "ideally run after A_ AAK lands"; now there's a *second* large reshaping (all of BB) on top. Auditing the foundry now means auditing a surface still actively moving under BB. AUAA5 should explicitly wait for BB to land — or move into/after it.

### 4. ₢AUAAh (regime-render sweep) — adjacent but distinct

BB's `fast-qualify-shellcheck-fold-cover-zero` (₢BBABG) reshapes `rbq_qualify_fast` and the marshal-zero gate. AUAAh's regime-render sweep is a *different* concern (render-function error hygiene, not shellcheck), so it can stay in AU — but if it integrates into `rbq_qualify_release` as the docket suggests, that orchestrator is being actively edited by BB. Light coupling, worth a glance at mount.

---

## What this means for ₣AU

This is a real "scope partly superseded" situation, not just stale prose. My read:

- **₢AUAAR should move to ₣BB** (or be sequenced as ₢BBAAH's explicit prerequisite). The prep-release ceremony shouldn't be co-owned by two heats editing the same file.
- **₢AUAA5 should wait for ₣BB to land** — re-scope its docket to audit the post-BB foundry, or restring it into BB's tail.
- **₢AUAA6 stays** but wants a docket refresh acknowledging BB's openssl sweep.
- **₢AUAAh, ₢AUAA7, ₢AUAA1, ₢AUAA4 stay** — they're genuinely AU-local (regime renders, onboarding walk, file move, BCG doc).

After those moves, ₣AU shrinks to a small cleanup-and-validation heat, and the paddock badly needs rewriting to drop the MVP-3/�the dead-₣As framing and stop claiming the release-qualification charter that ₣BB now holds.

These are all `restring`/`relocate`/paddock decisions — operator territory, so I haven't moved anything. Want me to:
1. **Restring ₢AUAAR into ₣BB** (and optionally ₢AUAA5), then
2. **Rewrite the ₣AU paddock** around the reduced scope?

Or would you rather look at the AUAAR/BBAAH relationship more closely first before deciding who owns the ceremony?

Proceeding. Three actions: relocate ₢AUAAR into ₣BB just ahead of the prep-release-integration pace (it's that pace's de-facto prerequisite), relocate ₢AUAA5 to ₣BB's tail (the foundry audit wants all of BB's reshaping landed first), then rewrite the ₣AU paddock around what remains.

I'll use `jjx_relocate` rather than `jjx_transfer` so I can place each precisely. Sequential, since each commits.

# jjezs_paddock ₣AU

## Paddock: rbk-14-mvp-release-finalize

## Context

A small cleanup-and-validation heat at the tail of the Recipe Bottle release
effort. Its original charter — supply-chain trust hardening plus
release-qualification tooling — has largely shipped or migrated. The
release-qualification charter now lives in the rbk-16 heat (₣BB): the
marshal-zero → gauntlet/pristine model, the RELEASE.md runbook, and the
prep-release ceremony all belong there. The prep-release ceremony rewrite and
the foundry/cloud-step trust audit that once lived here were restrung into ₣BB
so the release ceremony and the foundry surface each have a single owner.

What remains in ₣AU is genuinely local: developer-facing hygiene and end-to-end
validation that do not depend on the gauntlet model.

## Remaining Scope

- **Regime-render error hygiene** — every regime render/validate/info function
  should fail with an actionable parameter message, not a terse "module not
  kindled" crash or a set -u unbound-variable death. Includes a sweep test
  fixture that exercises every render/info and asserts clean exit, plus logging
  discipline for batch-sweep context.
- **Onboarding end-to-end walk** — exercise every handbook track in sequence
  from a learner's perspective; confirm windows render with prefixed resource
  names and probe outputs report the correct prefixed identifiers.
- **openssl base64 helper factoring** — the six base64 sites that ₣BB's base64
  sweep unified on openssl still carry a duplicated invocation; factor into
  rbgu_Utility.sh helpers, or close won't-fix with rationale recorded.
- **Small doc/file hygiene** — relocate the compose-probe sentinel out of the
  .rbk/ runtime config directory into Tools/rbk/ (the file move itself already
  appears done — verify references before closing); and a BCG clarification
  distinguishing the `$(<file)` builtin redirect from command substitution
  (verify against the landed substitution-rule refinement first — may already
  be covered).

## Relationship to other heats

- **₣BB (rbk-16-mvp-release-qualification)** — the live release-qualification
  heat; inherited this heat's prep-release ceremony work and the foundry trust
  audit. The audit is sequenced at ₣BB's tail so it reviews the post-reshaping
  foundry surface rather than transitional code.
- **₣Ak (rbk-mvp-1, retired)** — prior art for the directory/spec/test
  structure.

## Deferred technical debt (post-release itch candidates)

Catalogued during this heat's BCG-compliance work; deferred, not lost. Better
homed in jji_itch.md than in the paddock long-term:

- `z_` prefix on locals — naming convention, no runtime impact (pervasive)
- Unbraced `"$var"` expansions — mechanical
- `2>/dev/null` stderr suppression — case-by-case judgment
- `local -r` missing on single-assignment locals — pervasive
- Raw `echo` for user messages in display-heavy modules
- Bare file truncation (`> file` without `:`)

## References

- Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh — qualification orchestrator
- Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh — core `buv_render()` engine
- Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md — command-substitution rules
- Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh — utility home for the base64 helpers
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc — main specification
